items.author: 0xocdsec

items.content:
Today I remembered the story when I was based in China and the none-chines media company I worked for used Palo Alto Network's GlobalProtect as their corporate VPN and at some point we got a call from our Network security in London, that apparently a Chinese IP has connected to our internal network. 
After some thought and playing around turned out that Palo Alto was ( or still is ) using China based relays to get easier through the GFW for clients in China. When telling the network guys they were like "ah well, it is not an issue then. As you were." I still think about that occasionally.

items.author: 0xocdsec

items.content:
Memory never lies, it only disappears when the system powers off.

This screenshot shows a Volatility 3 memory forensics investigation against a Windows memory image (win11-24H2-memory.raw). At first glance, everything looks normal, but the deeper you dig, the more interesting it becomes. The process tree reveals an unusual executable (evil.exe) spawned through a suspicious parent process, while the malfind results identify executable memory pages with RWX permissions, a strong indicator of process injection or in-memory malware. YARA signatures have also matched suspicious regions, providing another layer of confidence that the process deserves further investigation.

What makes memory forensics so valuable is that it exposes artifacts you may never find on disk. Attackers increasingly rely on fileless techniques, reflective DLL injection, process hollowing, and credential theft that exist only in RAM. Even if the malware deletes itself from the filesystem, its process structures, loaded modules, command-line arguments, network connections, and injected memory regions often remain available until the system is powered off.

For incident responders, capturing memory before shutting down a compromised machine can be the difference between proving an intrusion and losing the most valuable evidence. Memory is one of the richest forensic artifacts available, and tools like Volatility continue to be indispensable for DFIR teams investigating modern attacks.

RT @visegrad24: Ursula von der Leyen announced yesterday that the EU will introduce a new mandatory EU age verification app for social media.

The new law proposal will be presented after the summer.

People creating new social media accounts will have to prove that they are above a certain age as the plans are to introduce social media bans for young people.

Users will need a passport, ID card or digital ID to generate “over X age” proofs for platforms.

For now, it seems that it will target new accounts but some are also pushing for  retroactive ID requirement to verify already existing social media accounts.

items.author: ipurple

items.content:
RT @jespinog: "The VFS" is the fifth chapter of the Understanding the Linux Kernel series, and it answers a question that's easy to never ask: how can file operations work identically on an ext4 file, a /proc file that exists only as kernel memory, and an NFS share on another machine?

The answer is a classic: program against an interface, not an implementation. The VFS defines four objects, each with a table of function pointers: the superblock (one mounted filesystem), the inode (one file or directory), the dentry (one cached name), and the file (one open instance). A filesystem plugs in by filling those tables of pointers.

Read it here: https://t.co/8Kp5FswvGq

items.author: zuhaitz_dev

items.content:
RT @ippsec: It's really crazy to think about when I was in school, we could play a game called Oregon Trail. Essentially an RPG where you travel across the US and figure out which of your friends dies of horrible diseases like Dysentery.

The crazy part here is we are in a computer lab, not knowing what Dysentery was. So we had to leave the computer,  go to the library, find the book and look it up. Nowadays, my phone may accidentally think I'm asking it a question and tell me the answer "unprompted". People underestimate just how accessible information is nowadays.

Also wtf. Looking back, I'm sure the computer had a built-in dictionary... Was that the teacher's cruel joke of "if you knew how to use a computer, you wouldn't need to use the library?" lol. Did I miss the lesson completely because I thought it was leisure time?

items.author: nodotbtree

items.content:
If you are building offensive tooling, writing detection rules for C2 implants, or analysing modern malware evasion, this blog covers the techniques being used right now.

Position-independent shellcode architecture. Modular PIC C2 agents. Crystal Palace API and Crystal Kit for composable tradecraft. Aspect-Oriented Programming for PIC. Module stomping. Hook chains. Sleep masking and BeaconGate. UDRL reflective loading. Beacon Object Files and BOF Cocktails. AMSI bypass via memory patching. YARA rule generation from binary invariants. Custom beacon artifacts. Token impersonation. C2 redirectors. Infrastructure as code with Terraform and Ansible.

Built on top of Raphael Mudge’s Tradecraft Garden and Crystal Palace, extending and stress-testing the framework in practice. Understanding how these modular PIC implants are built and composed is how you detect them.

Also the creator of Red Team Ops 1/2 and CRTO/CRTL, one of the most respected red team certifications in the industry.

The Big Cheese: https://t.co/0v4TjeMrrK

Crystal Kit: https://t.co/dHWf5bWcE3

Author: @_RastaMouse 

#RedTeam #DetectionEngineering #InfoSec

items.author: 0xocdsec

items.content:
RT @cr3ghost: The creator of Cobalt Strike left the industry in 2021, came back, and is now publishing everything he knows about evasion tradecraft openly and for free.

Tradecraft Garden separates evasion tradecraft from capability. Crystal Palace is the linker that makes it work: position-independent code, binary transformation, code and register randomization, link-time hooking, YARA rule generation from invariant instructions, and a PICO convention for reusable tradecraft modules.

The community built an entire ecosystem on top of it. Crystal Kit for Cobalt Strike, Sliver, Mythic, and Adaptix. Reflective loaders, call stack spoofing, sleep masking, module overloading. Offense and defense both benefit because every technique is published as a testable ground truth.

If you work in red teaming, detection engineering, or EDR evaluation, this is required reading.

TTPs: https://t.co/A4O6Tuh58M 
Blog: https://t.co/4jb7cRpcA9

His original Red Team Ops with Cobalt Strike series is also still on YouTube. 9 parts covering the full red team operations workflow. Free. From the person who built the tool. Red Team Ops with Cobalt Strike - Operations (1 of 9): https://t.co/Jd28yWuaYs

Author: Raphael Mudge

#BlueTeam #InfoSec #RedTeam

items.content: RT @0xpwnie: A famous example of this is the Level 256 of the original Pac-Man arcade game. It tracked the current level using an 8-bit integer(max value 255). Whenever a player completed Level 255, the game added 1. The counter overflowed and rolled back to 0. The game's code wasn't prepared for a 'Level 0', causing it to draw 256 fruit items on the screen, corrupting the right half of the screen and making the level impossible to beat:)

items.content:
RT @advance_sec0: 🚨 Attention Security Researchers, Ethical Hackers, Exploit Developers & Bug Bounty Hunters 🚨

We are currently seeking high-quality, reliable 0-day exploits and zero-day vulnerabilities across several high-value targets.

Our current focus areas include:

• SonicWall SSL-VPN appliances and OS7/NSA series
• Citrix NetScaler
• Palo Alto Networks VPN solutions
• Pulse Secure / Ivanti VPN
• Fortinet SSL-VPN
• Local Privilege Escalation (LPE) and Remote Code Execution (RCE) on Windows, macOS, and Linux
• Microsoft Office suite vulnerabilities

We are also actively interested in vulnerabilities affecting IoT devices, routers, embedded systems, and other network infrastructure.

Whether you’re an independent researcher, part of a small team, or a bug bounty hunter with private/non-public findings, we welcome submissions of functional, high-quality exploits (including chains where relevant).

What we offer:

Competitive compensation for accepted submissions
Strict confidentiality and professional handling of your work

Straightforward and respectful communication

If you have relevant exploits ready, please reach out:

📧 Email: submit@advance-sec.com
💬 Wire: Advance_sec

Help us connect with more talented researchers in the community — likes, comments, and shares are greatly appreciated!

#0day #ZeroDay #ZeroDayExploit #ExploitDev #VulnerabilityResearch #EthicalHacking #InfoSec #CyberSecurity #BugBounty #PenetrationTesting #RedTeam #NetworkSecurity #IoTSecurity #VPN #RouterSecurity #SecurityResearch #Exploit #Vuln

items.content:
RT @HexRaysSA: 📢 IDA 9.4 is here! 
Huge thanks to our beta testers for spending the last several weeks refining this release. 

• The Apple Dyld Shared Cache workflow has been rebuilt from the ground up. 
• The decompiler now speaks Swift, with proper ABI modelling for self, async context, and error paths. 
• Two new processor modules land — Qualcomm Hexagon and MCore. 
• Navigation gets a major upgrade with Pathfinder and a redesigned Jump Anywhere. 
• The Teams add-on now runs on Git. 
• And idalib, previously Pro-only, now ships with IDA Home. 

👉 Read the blog for the full breakdown and/or jump ahead to the release notes, then grab your update in the Download Center.
https://t.co/hbo93SWxTM

items.author: artem_i_baranov

items.content:
RT @ptdbugs: A PoC/exploit has been discovered for vulnerability in Firefox 152.0.5

Vendor: Mozilla Foundation 
Product: Mozilla Firefox
Description: Firefox 152.0.5 on Windows contains a chain of flaws that together allow full remote code execution from a single malicious webpage visit. A memory-safety issue in the WebAssembly/Baseline-JIT engine permits an attacker to break out of the browser's content-process sandbox into a privileged internal browser context. From there, insufficient origin/authorization checks in Firefox's profile-backup restore feature allow a maliciously crafted backup archive to be accepted and restored without proper validation, including an attacker-controlled NSS (cryptography module) configuration. On restart, NSS loads a library path from this restored configuration without verifying its authenticity, resulting in arbitrary code execution under the logged-in user's privileges. No user interaction beyond visiting a webpage is required, and no prior access, credentials, or privileges are needed — making this an unauthenticated, network-exploitable, one-click RCE chain.

Link: https://t.co/zBGxcSuBZH
items.author: 0xocdsec

items.content:
RT @ptdbugs: A PoC/exploit has been discovered for vulnerability in OpenSSH 10.4p1

Vendor: OpenBSD
Product: OpenSSH 
Description: Portable OpenSSH's ssh-agent (affecting versions from 9.3p2 through at least 10.4p1) contains a state-tracking flaw in how it classifies forwarded versus local connections when the agent is locked and later unlocked. Because the session-bind@openssh.com extension used to mark a socket as "remote" is discarded outright while the agent is locked, a forwarded agent socket that remains open across a lock/unlock cycle is subsequently misclassified as local. This allows a party with access to that forwarded socket — typically an SSH server the victim connected to with agent forwarding enabled — to submit a PKCS#11 provider-loading request that the agent's remote-provider restriction was specifically designed to block, resulting in the agent loading and initializing an attacker-influenced module. Exploitation requires the victim to use agent forwarding to an attacker-influenced host, lock and later unlock their agent while that forwarded connection remains open, and a PKCS#11 provider path permitted by the agent's allowlist.

Link: https://t.co/AihqL5e7d7

items.author: ipurple

items.content:
RT @ThruntingLabs: Most security training starts by telling you what happened.

Then it asks you to find the evidence.

Threat Hunting Labs works the other way around.

You begin with an alert, a small amount of context, and access to the telemetry.

From there, you have to:

• Form a hypothesis
• Write the queries
• Follow the evidence
• Decide what happened
• Explain how you reached that conclusion

The goal is not to memorize attacker techniques.

It is to become better at investigating them.

See how it works and start with free access: https://t.co/9sDdyIFHwE
